message:
fix(fcp-webhook): reject malleable Ed25519 signatures via verify_strict (br-r3ygj)

Closes flywheel_connectors-r3ygj (P1/HIGH).

Reviewer (cod_3 P5) flagged that fcp-webhook's Ed25519Verifier::verify
used ed25519_dalek::Verifier::verify, which does NOT reject:

  * Signatures with S in the upper half of the curve order
    (S >= L = 2^252 + 27742317777372353535851937790883648493).
    RFC 8032 §5.1.7 says verifiers SHOULD reject such S.
  * Mixed-order public-key points.

Both permit bytewise-distinct-but-valid signatures over the same
payload, so a webhook receiver that keys downstream dedupe / audit /
idempotency-cache on the signature encoding gets a malleability
attack for free. The rest of the workspace (fcp-crypto, fcp-bootstrap,
etc.) already uses verify_strict on untrusted inputs; fcp-webhook is
on the same untrusted boundary and should match.

Fix: single-line change — swap verify for verify_strict at signature.rs:
219. Added an explanatory comment citing RFC 8032 §5.1.7 and the
workspace-wide verify_strict convention.

Regression test test_ed25519_rejects_malleable_high_s_signature:
1. Signs a payload normally.
2. Constructs the malleable variant by little-endian adding L into the
   S half of the signature (S' = S + L mod 2^256); asserts the S bytes
   actually changed and no overflow occurred.
3. Asserts the canonical signature still verifies (positive control).
4. Asserts verify returns InvalidSignature on the malleable variant.

Before the fix this test fails (both canonical and S+L verify). After
the fix, only the canonical one verifies.

Verified: cargo test -p fcp-webhook — 573/573 pass (465 lib + 102
integration + 6 no_mock + 1 ignored + my new test).

Cod_4 / SunnyMoose
